Netherlands.qualification_recognition:
QUALIFICATION RECOGNITION — NETHERLANDS

The Netherlands is Europe's most English-friendly study destination with over 2,000 English-taught bachelor's programmes — more than any other non-English-speaking country. You do NOT need to learn Dutch to study here if you choose English-taught programmes.

TWO TYPES OF INSTITUTIONS:
Research University (WO/Universiteit): Deep theoretical knowledge. Higher entry requirements. For Medicine, Law, Sciences, Engineering. Entry requires VWO equivalent qualification.
University of Applied Sciences (HBO/Hogeschool): Practical, career-ready degrees. Internship-heavy. Lower entry requirements. Entry requires HAVO equivalent qualification.
Numerus Fixus: Limited-seat programmes — ALL medicine programmes and some others (Psychology, Physiotherapy, some business programmes) have a HARD DEADLINE of 15 January. No exceptions. Missing this means waiting a full year.

APPLICATION SYSTEM:
Studielink (studielink.nl): National online portal. Apply to up to 4 programmes simultaneously. Register from October.
IMPORTANT: Most universities require ADDITIONAL application steps on their own portal after Studielink registration. Check each university's own system.
Visa/Residence Permit: Your university handles the MVV and residence permit application as your SPONSOR — you do NOT apply through an embassy initially. Process takes 2.5-3.5 months. Apply non-EU deadline: 1 April (recommended to allow time for permit processing).

NUMERUS FIXUS DEADLINE — CRITICAL:
All Medicine, Psychology, Physiotherapy at most universities: 15 January HARD DEADLINE. No late applications. No exceptions. Missing it means waiting a full year.
Regular programmes: 1 May for EU/EEA. 1 April recommended for non-EU (visa processing time).

WHICH QUALIFICATIONS ARE ACCEPTED:
IB DIPLOMA: Directly equivalent to VWO. Minimum 28 points typically for WO. Competitive programmes higher. Fully accepted for HBO.
CAMBRIDGE A-LEVELS (2-3 subjects + GCSEs): Recognised as VWO equivalent. Grade requirements vary by programme.
INDIAN CBSE / ISC CLASS 12 (5 subjects): Generally accepted — typically 75%+ overall for WO. Some programmes require Foundation Year. Check per university.
PAKISTANI CAMBRIDGE A-LEVELS: Same as UK A-Levels.
PAKISTANI FSc (HSSC): HAVO level typically — may require Foundation Year for WO. Generally accepted for HBO.
SRI LANKA A/L: Generally accepted for WO.
UAE A-LEVELS / IB (INTERNATIONAL SCHOOL): Same as standard A-Levels/IB.
UAE TAWJIHI: May be considered HAVO level — check per university. Foundation Year may be needed for WO.
US HIGH SCHOOL DIPLOMA + SAT: With SAT 1200+ or 3+ AP exams at 4-5, accepted for WO at most universities.
FOUNDATION YEAR: 1-year preparatory programme. Many Dutch universities guarantee entry to bachelor's programme upon completion. Cost: €8,000-15,000. Providers: Leiden, Utrecht, Groningen, UvA, INTO, Study Group, Kaplan, Navitas.
IGCSE ALONE: Not sufficient. Must complete A-Levels or IB before applying.
No nationality-specific pre-process like APS (Germany) or Campus France (France) required for Dutch applications.

TWO-GATE SYSTEM:
Gate 1 — Eligibility: Certificate equivalent to VWO (WO) or HAVO (HBO) as assessed by Nuffic + individual university. Check at Nuffic (nuffic.nl) or studyinnl.org.
Gate 2 — Competitive: Grades, language test, motivation letter, aptitude test results (Numerus Fixus), matching/interview performance.

ENGLISH LANGUAGE REQUIREMENTS:
IELTS Academic: 6.0-6.5 overall (no band below 5.5) standard. 6.5-7.0 for Medicine/competitive.
TOEFL iBT: 80-90 standard. 90-100 for competitive.
PTE Academic: 58-63 standard. 65-76 for competitive.
Exemptions: English-medium international school — confirm with each university. UK/USA/Canada/Australia/NZ/Ireland nationals. IB English A (first language) — check with each university. Even from English-medium school, many Dutch universities still require formal IELTS/TOEFL.
Dutch language: NT2-II certification (CEFR B2) only required for Dutch-taught programmes — most international students avoid this by choosing English-taught programmes.

FINANCIAL PROOF:
Approximately €13,200 for the full year required as financial proof.
MVV and residence permit: Handled by university as your sponsor after acceptance. University submits on your behalf. Processing 2.5-3.5 months after admission letter.
Housing warning: Netherlands has a serious student housing shortage — especially Amsterdam, Utrecht, Leiden, Delft. Begin housing search 6 months before arrival. Apply for university housing immediately upon accepting offer.
Netherlands.admission_checklist:
APPLICATION PORTAL — STUDIELINK
Studielink (studielink.nl) is the national application portal. Apply to up to 4 programmes simultaneously. IMPORTANT: Most universities require ADDITIONAL documents uploaded through their OWN portal after Studielink registration — you will receive an email invitation. Check each university website for secondary steps. Application fee: €75–100 non-refundable (non-EU students at most universities). EU students may use DigiD; non-EU create account directly.

CRITICAL DEADLINES
Numerus Fixus programmes (Medicine, Psychology, some others): 15 January — HARD DEADLINE. No exceptions, no late applications. Missing this means waiting a full year. Regular programmes: 1 April recommended for non-EU (to allow visa processing time). EU/EEA: 1 May.

NUMERUS FIXUS SELECTION PROCEDURE
Limited-seat programmes use multi-criteria selection. Timeline: apply by 15 January → selection procedure January–April → ranking number issued 15 April → offer/rejection. Criteria vary per university: weighted grades, motivation letter, aptitude tests, sometimes interviews. Matching procedure: some programmes (especially UvA) require a mandatory matching activity — online questionnaire or campus event. For Numerus Fixus this is eliminatory. For regular programmes it is advisory.

ENGLISH LANGUAGE REQUIREMENTS
IELTS Academic: 6.0–6.5 standard; 6.5–7.0 for selective programmes. TOEFL iBT: 80–90 standard. Cambridge Advanced: C grade. Dutch NOT required for English-taught programmes.

MVV AND RESIDENCE PERMIT — UNIVERSITY SPONSORS YOU
Dutch visa process is unique: your university applies for your MVV (entry visa) and residence permit on your behalf as sponsor — you do NOT apply through the embassy initially. This is the TEV (Toegang en Verblijf) procedure. Processing time: approximately 2.5–3.5 months after receiving admission letter. After arrival: register at local municipality for BSN (citizen service number) within 5 days of arrival — mandatory for banking, insurance, healthcare, and all official purposes.

HOUSING — CRITICAL WARNING
The Netherlands has a serious student housing shortage — particularly Amsterdam, Utrecht, Leiden, Delft. University housing limited and oversubscribed. Private rental expensive: €700–1,500/month for a room in Amsterdam. Start housing search 6 months before arrival. Do NOT wait for admission letter — begin researching immediately. Apply for university housing the day you accept your offer. Resources: SSH student housing, ROOM.nl, Kamernet, university housing portals.

FINANCIAL PROOF
Non-EU tuition fees: €9,000–20,000/year (research universities). Living costs: approximately €900–1,300/month. Holland Scholarship: €5,000 one-time for first year — apply through individual universities, deadlines typically February–May.

POST-STUDY — ZOEKJAAR VISA
1-year Orientation Year Visa after graduation to find work or start a business. Can convert to highly skilled migrant visa (Kennismigrant) after finding qualifying employment.

OFFICIAL SOURCES FOR VERIFICATION
studielink.nl — application portal
nuffic.nl — Dutch qualification recognition
ind.nl — residence permit (IND immigration service)
studyinholland.nl — official Dutch study information
hollandscholarship.com — Holland Scholarship

France.qualification_recognition:
QUALIFICATION RECOGNITION — FRANCE

France has TWO parallel education systems:
Public Universities (Universités): Open admission, very low fees (~€2,770-3,770/year for non-EU). Licence (3yr), Master, Doctorat structure. Wide subject range.
Grandes Écoles: Elite selective institutions — engineering (CentraleSupélec, INSA, Mines), business (HEC Paris, ESSEC, ESCP), political science (Sciences Po). Much more competitive. Often higher fees.

THREE APPLICATION SYSTEMS — NATIONALITY DETERMINES WHICH ONE:
This is where France is most different from other countries. There is NO single platform for everyone.

PARCOURSUP:
For: EU/EEA students, French Baccalauréat holders, UK nationals (post-Brexit), non-EU students holding French Bac from French school abroad.
Platform: parcoursup.gouv.fr. Deadline: January 19 - March 12 (for 2026 entry).

ÉTUDES EN FRANCE:
For: Non-EU students in 73 countries — India, UAE, Egypt, Pakistan, Bangladesh, Gulf countries, most of Asia, Africa, Americas.
Mandatory pre-step: Must register with Campus France office in home country FIRST before any university receives your application. Cannot apply directly to French universities without this for Licence 1 admission.
Campus France India: india.campusfrance.org. UAE: ae.campusfrance.org. Egypt: egypte.campusfrance.org. Pakistan: pk.campusfrance.org.
Platform: pastel.diplomatie.gouv.fr. Applications open 1 October. Close approximately 19 January. Maximum 3 university choices — choose carefully, cannot change after submission.
Campus France registration fee: typically €60-100. Pre-departure interview required in some countries.
Process takes 4-8 weeks minimum. Start in October at the latest for September entry.

DIRECT APPLICATION:
For: Grandes Écoles applications (most have own admissions process). Arts schools (CampusArt platform). Students in countries NOT covered by Études en France.

QUALIFICATION RECOGNITION:
A-LEVELS, IB DIPLOMA, CBSE, AP + US DIPLOMA, FSc, HSSC, Sri Lanka A/L, UAE/Gulf international school qualifications: All accepted as equivalent to the French Baccalauréat through Campus France/Études en France assessment. No separate French equivalency database like Germany's anabin.
IGCSE ALONE: Not sufficient. Must complete A-Levels or IB Diploma before applying.
For Grandes Écoles direct international admission: A-Levels/IB + GMAT 650+ + IELTS 7.0/TOEFL 100 + motivation letter + interview typically required.

LANGUAGE REQUIREMENTS:
French-taught programmes (majority of public university Licence programmes):
MINIMUM B2 level — DELF B2 or TCF DAP (400 points minimum). For Grandes Écoles and selective programmes: C1 required — DALF C1.
Timeline: Reaching B2 in French from zero takes approximately 600-800 hours — roughly 2 years of consistent learning. Start no later than 2 years before intended entry.
Alliance Française and Institut Français have offices in most countries including India, UAE, Egypt.

English-taught programmes (growing — especially Grandes Écoles and private business schools):
IELTS Academic: 6.5 minimum for most business schools. 7.0 for top schools (HEC Paris, ESSEC).
TOEFL iBT: 90-100 for most. 100-110 for top Grandes Écoles.
Public universities increasingly offer English-taught Licence programmes — mainly in Paris and major cities.

MEDICINE IN FRANCE — COMPLETELY DIFFERENT:
France reformed medical admission in 2020. First year is now PASS (Parcours Accès Santé Spécifique) or L.AS (Licence avec Accès Santé) — highly competitive first years with only a small percentage advancing to Year 2 of Medicine. Unlike UK (direct MBBS entry) or Germany (direct entry), France uses a competitive selection WITHIN the first university year. You cannot know in advance if you will progress. This is a critical distinction.

VISA AND FINANCIAL REQUIREMENTS:
VLS-TS (Visa Long Séjour valant Titre de Séjour): Long-stay student visa for France. Valid 1 year, automatically serves as residence permit. Must be validated with OFII within 3 months of arrival.
APS post-study work permit: 1 year after graduation. Can be extended or converted to work visa.
Financial proof: Typically €615/month living costs required (€7,380/year) — check current French government requirements.
France.admission_checklist:
APPLICATION SYSTEMS — THREE ROUTES
Route 1 — Études en France (non-EU students from 73 countries including India, UAE, Egypt, Pakistan, Gulf countries): Apply online through Études en France platform managed by Campus France. Maximum 3 university choices. Deadline: approximately 19 January — verify current year on campusfrance.org. MANDATORY FIRST STEP: Register with Campus France office in your home country BEFORE applying. Application fee: varies by country (typically €50–100).
Route 2 — Parcoursup: For EU/EEA students and specific cases only. Not applicable for most non-EU international students.
Route 3 — Direct to Grandes Écoles: Most prestigious business and engineering Grandes Écoles have their own international application portals. Apply directly — NOT through Études en France. Deadlines: typically November–February for September entry. Sciences Po has its own dedicated international admissions portal (sciencespo.fr) — NOT through Études en France.

CAMPUS FRANCE REGISTRATION — MANDATORY FIRST STEP
Register with Campus France office in your country BEFORE submitting Études en France application — mandatory step. India offices: New Delhi, Mumbai, Chennai, Bangalore, Hyderabad, Kolkata. UAE: ae.campusfrance.org. Processing time: allow 2–4 weeks for Campus France interview and validation.

FRENCH LANGUAGE REQUIREMENTS
For French-taught programmes: DELF B2 minimum (issued by French Ministry — valid for life, no expiry). DALF C1 strongly recommended for competitive programmes including Medicine. TCF DAP: 400 points minimum (alternative to DELF). Time to reach B2 from zero: approximately 18–24 months.

ENGLISH LANGUAGE REQUIREMENTS
IELTS Academic: 6.5 minimum for English-taught programmes. Grandes Écoles business schools: often require GMAT 650+ additionally. TAGE MAGE accepted by some French business schools as alternative to GMAT — check per school.

STUDENT VISA — VLS-TS
Apply at French Embassy or Consulate after receiving acceptance letter. Documents: acceptance letter, Campus France registration confirmation, financial proof, accommodation proof, health insurance, passport, passport photos. Visa fee: approximately €99 (2025). Visa type: VLS-TS (long-stay visa serving as residence permit). CRITICAL: Must validate VLS-TS with OFII (French immigration office) within 3 months of arrival in France — mandatory, failure invalidates right to remain.

FINANCIAL PROOF
Approximately €615/month minimum (official). Paris budget: €1,000–1,500/month. Tuition fees: public universities €2,770–3,770/year non-EU. Grandes Écoles: €5,000–20,000/year.

POST-STUDY WORK
APS (Autorisation Provisoire de Séjour): 1-year post-study work permit for graduates from French institutions. Can be renewed or converted to a work visa. Available to non-EU graduates.

ARTS SCHOOLS — SEPARATE SYSTEM
Apply through CampusArt (campusart.org) for approximately 100 French art schools. Registration opens 1 October, closes 28 February. Portfolio is the primary selection criterion.

OFFICIAL SOURCES FOR VERIFICATION
campusfrance.org — Campus France national portal
etudes-en-france.campusfrance.org — Études en France application
service-public.fr — French government services
ofii.fr — OFII visa validation
campusart.org — arts school applications

Canada.qualification_recognition:
QUALIFICATION RECOGNITION — CANADA

CANADA IS DECENTRALISED — CRITICAL UNDERSTANDING:
Canadian admissions are NOT centralised. Requirements, deadlines, English-language rules, supplemental forms, tuition, and immigration requirements vary by province, university, faculty, and educational background. Always verify every final requirement on the specific university and programme page.

STUDENT STATUS — TWO SEPARATE AXES:
Legal status and educational background are NOT the same thing. This is the most misunderstood aspect of Canadian admissions.
Domestic (Citizen/PR): Canadian citizen or permanent resident. Eligible for domestic fees. BUT may still be evaluated as a foreign-curriculum applicant if they studied abroad — e.g. a Canadian citizen who studied CBSE in Dubai is domestic by status but assessed under foreign educational background category.
Never assume a Canadian passport means Ontario-style admission rules apply.
International (Study Permit required): Must handle both admission AND immigration. University offer first, then DLI/PAL/study permit compliance.

APPLICATION PLATFORMS BY PROVINCE:
Ontario: OUAC (Ontario Universities Application Centre) — ouac.on.ca. Most Ontario universities use this. Group B applicants include international students, out-of-province Canadians, transfer students.
British Columbia: Direct to each university (UBC, SFU, UVIC etc.) — no provincial portal.
Quebec: Direct to each institution. French-language requirements may apply at Université de Montréal, UQAM, Laval.
Other provinces: Direct to each university — check individual university websites.
Do NOT assume Ontario timelines or forms apply nationally.

WHICH QUALIFICATIONS ARE ACCEPTED:
IB DIPLOMA: Widely recognised across all Canadian universities. High HL grades may earn college credit.
CAMBRIDGE A-LEVELS: Accepted at all Canadian universities. Treated similarly to UK A-Levels.
INDIAN CBSE / ISC CLASS 12: Accepted. Top-performing students are competitive at all Canadian universities including Toronto, UBC, McGill.
PAKISTANI CAMBRIDGE A-LEVELS: Accepted — same as standard A-Levels.
PAKISTANI FSc: Accepted — check per university for specific grade requirements.
US HIGH SCHOOL DIPLOMA + SAT/AP: Accepted. Canadian universities are familiar with US curriculum.
UAE / GULF INTERNATIONAL SCHOOL (A-LEVELS OR IB): Fully accepted.
GULF NATIONAL CURRICULA (TAWJIHI, EMSAT ETC.): Check per university — some may require foundation year or additional documentation.
IGCSE ALONE: Not sufficient. Must complete A-Levels, IB, or equivalent Grade 12 qualification before applying.

MEDICINE AND LAW — IMPORTANT:
Medicine in Canada is a GRADUATE-LEVEL programme (MD), not undergraduate. Students aiming for medicine must first complete an undergraduate degree (typically 3-4 years of science/health sciences), then apply to medical school. No direct undergraduate medicine entry in Canada.
Law in Canada is a GRADUATE-LEVEL programme (JD), typically pursued after a bachelor's degree. McGill Law also requires evidence of passive bilingualism in English and French. Undergraduate law-related options (legal studies, criminology, political science) are NOT the same as qualifying to practise law.

SUPPLEMENTAL APPLICATIONS:
Many competitive programmes require supplemental applications beyond the main application. University of Toronto and Waterloo use supplementary processes for competitive programmes (Engineering, Commerce, Computer Science etc.). Always check if a supplemental form or additional information is required for each specific programme.

ENGLISH LANGUAGE REQUIREMENTS:
Never assume exemption because the school was English-medium. Universities set their own rules — exemption is institution-specific. Some may waive testing for certain school systems but many still require explicit proof.
Typical minimums: IELTS 6.5-7.0 overall. TOEFL iBT 90-100. PTE 65+.
French: At Université de Montréal, UQAM, Laval, and French-medium programmes at McGill — French proficiency required (typically DELF B2 or higher).

STUDY PERMIT AND IMMIGRATION:
Study Permit: Required for all international students. Apply after receiving university admission offer from a Designated Learning Institution (DLI).
PAL/TAL (Provincial/Territorial Attestation Letter): Many post-secondary applicants need this before filing for study permit. Check if your province and programme requires it.
Proof of funds: Must show sufficient funds for tuition, living costs, and return travel — check current federal Government of Canada requirements as amounts change.
PGWP (Post-Graduation Work Permit): Available after graduation from eligible DLI. Duration linked to programme length. Students must verify DLI eligibility and PGWP rules — not all institutions or programmes qualify equally. Critical for immigration pathway planning.
Canada.admission_checklist:
APPLICATION PORTALS — DECENTRALISED SYSTEM
Canada has NO single national undergraduate application portal. Ontario universities: most applications through OUAC (ouac.on.ca). Outside Ontario: apply directly to each university's own portal — no central system. Application fee: CAD 90–200 per university depending on province and institution. Cannot assume Ontario rules or timelines apply nationally. Each province and university has its own deadlines — verify each individually. Ontario main cycle deadline: typically January for September entry. Outside Ontario: varies by university, often January–March.

APPLICANT CATEGORIES — CRITICAL FIRST STEP
Canada classifies applicants by both legal status AND educational background — these are separate questions. Legal status: domestic (Canadian citizen or PR), or international (study permit required). Educational background: Ontario high school, Canadian outside Ontario, US curriculum, high school outside Canada and US (covers IB, A-Levels, CBSE, Gulf curricula etc.), transfer student, mature student. Your admission requirements depend on BOTH categories — never assume domestic status means Ontario curriculum rules apply.

CREDENTIAL EVALUATION — WHEN REQUIRED
Most Canadian universities assess CBSE, IB, A-Levels, and other international qualifications DIRECTLY — a third-party credential evaluation is NOT universally required for Grade 12 applicants. However: some universities may request a WES (World Education Services) evaluation or equivalent ECA for students from certain countries, especially for transcripts less familiar to the admissions team. Check each university's international admissions page. WES is primarily required for immigration purposes (PR applications, work permits) AFTER graduation — not automatically needed for undergraduate admission.

SUPPLEMENTAL REQUIREMENTS — COMMONLY MISSED
Many competitive Canadian programmes require supplemental review IN ADDITION to grades. No entrance exam nationally — selective programmes substitute essays, short-answer questions, video responses, personal profiles, portfolios, auditions, or interviews. University of Toronto: supplemental applications for some programmes including short-answer, essay, and video responses. Waterloo: supplemental information forms through applicant portal. Concordia: portfolios, auditions, letters of intent for many programmes. Missing a supplemental component ends your application even with strong grades.

ENGLISH LANGUAGE REQUIREMENTS
Never assume exemption because your school was English-medium — verify with each specific university. IELTS Academic: 6.5 standard; 7.0 for competitive programmes. TOEFL iBT: 90–100 standard. Some universities accept Duolingo English Test. Exemption policies vary — confirm on each university admissions page.

QUEBEC — LANGUAGE OF INSTRUCTION WARNING
Most Quebec universities teach EXCLUSIVELY in French: Université de Montréal, Université Laval, UQAM, and others. English-medium Quebec universities: McGill University and Concordia University only. Students who do not speak French should only target McGill or Concordia in Quebec. McGill BCL/JD law programme additionally requires proof of passive bilingualism in English AND French — this is a hard requirement even for English speakers.

PAL/TAL — PROVINCIAL ATTESTATION LETTER
Many international post-secondary applicants need a Provincial Attestation Letter (PAL) or Territorial Attestation Letter (TAL) from the province BEFORE applying for a study permit. Verify current rules at ircc.canada.ca. PAL is province-issued — contact the provincial government of the province where your university is located. Without PAL/TAL where required, study permit application cannot proceed.

STUDY PERMIT PROCEDURE
Apply via IRCC online portal at ircc.canada.ca. Required documents: letter of acceptance from DLI (Designated Learning Institution), PAL/TAL where required, proof of financial support (tuition + CAD 10,000/year living costs minimum), valid passport, biometrics at a Visa Application Centre (VAC) near you. Processing time: several months — begin planning immediately after receiving acceptance. CRITICAL: Verify your university is a DLI before applying — check DLI list at ircc.canada.ca. Not all institutions qualify equally for PGWP purposes. Work rights during study: up to 20 hours/week on campus during term; full-time during scheduled breaks.

HEALTH INSURANCE — PROVINCE SPECIFIC
No single national health insurance for international students. Ontario OHIP (provincial health) has a 3-month waiting period for new arrivals — not covered immediately on arrival. Purchase your university student health plan before arrival — covers you during the waiting period. Other provinces: coverage rules vary. Always purchase university health insurance plan regardless of province.

FINANCIAL PROOF
Minimum: full first year tuition PLUS CAD 10,000 living expenses (federal minimum — actual costs higher). Tuition: CAD 25,000–55,000/year for international students depending on programme and province. Living costs vary significantly by city: Toronto and Vancouver most expensive (CAD 15,000–20,000/year living). Atlantic provinces and prairies significantly cheaper.

LAW — DIFFERENT FROM UK MODEL
Professional law in Canada is NOT direct entry from Grade 12 in most cases — unlike UK (LLB) or India (BA LLB). Most Canadian law schools (JD programmes) require a prior bachelor's degree. Students planning a law career should first complete an undergraduate degree in any subject. McGill BCL/JD is a partial exception but requires at least 60 university credits OR Quebec CEGEP completion PLUS proof of passive bilingualism in English AND French.

PGWP — POST-GRADUATION WORK PERMIT
Up to 3 years work permit after graduation depending on programme length. Only available to graduates of eligible DLI institutions. Verify PGWP eligibility of your specific programme and institution BEFORE applying — not all institutions or programmes qualify equally. Key pathway to Canadian permanent residency.

OFFICIAL SOURCES FOR VERIFICATION
ouac.on.ca — Ontario Universities Application Centre
ircc.canada.ca — study permit, PAL/TAL, PGWP guidance
educanada.ca — official Canadian government study portal
wes.org — WES credential evaluation (primarily for post-graduation immigration)
ouinfo.ca — Ontario university programme information
